original_abstract:
International organizations (IOs) frequently endure long after their initial founding, yet our understanding of how they navigate shifting domestic politics, global geopolitical realignments, and internal institutional pressures remains fragmented. This special issue introduces a "life cycle" perspective to the study of international cooperation, examining the inherent ebbs and flows of the institutions that anchor the global order. While disciplines such as firm theory, organizational sociology, and agency theory have long utilized life cycle frameworks, international relations has only begun to harness their potential. 

A life cycle approach shifts the analytical focus toward the IO itself as a dynamic entity, tracing evolutionary stages that include false starts, consolidation, inertia, expansion, decline, and eventual succession or death. By centering these processes, this issue highlights that institutional history is defined by alternating periods of vitality and stagnation. Understanding the mechanisms that drive these transitions is essential for assessing the overall resilience of the international system. The contributing articles investigate how IOs maintain durability through crises, the strategies used to reclaim legitimacy, the influence of leadership rhetoric, and the strategic trade-offs member states face when deciding whether to reform an existing institution or establish a new one. Furthermore, the collection explores the systemic consequences of member-state withdrawal and the public perceptions that shape these foundational disruptions.
humanized_abstract:
International organizations (IOs) often endure for many decades if not centuries after their founding. However, there is still relatively little research on how they cope with a range of challenges including the pressures of domestic politics, shifting global patterns of power and influence, and internally within the organization itself. This special issue offers the first collection of articles to apply a life cycle framework to the study of international cooperation, while documenting the dramatic events and dynamics that shape institutions at the heart of the international system. The life cycle framework has long been employed by firm theory, organizational sociology and agency theory, but in international relations the use of the life cycle perspective is in its infancy.

The life cycle approach views the International Organisation (IO) as an entity whose evolutional stages are studied in detail. In the various stages there may be initial failure and gradual development, as well as stagnation, expansion, regression, and extinction. This issue illustrates in more detail than usual that the history of international institutions is composed of periods of stability and dynamism. In order to obtain information on the mechanisms that explain this dynamism and their effect on the system level, the articles included in this issue study among other things how international organisations come through crises, how lost legitimacy can be regained, the impact of speeches of leading officials on the audiences to which they are addressed, and the calculation that members have to carry out in the decision whether to try to reform the institution and invest more efforts in it, or whether to break away and establish a new organisation. Finally, this issue also contains studies on the consequences of the decisions made by members, as well as the opinions of the general public in relation to the foundation of an international institution.
